questions.question:
A line of symmetry is defined as a line that divides a figure into two parts in a way such that each part is a mirror image of the other part about that line. The given figure consists of 35 unit squares arranged as shown. In addition to the three black squares, what is the minimum number of squares that must be coloured black so that both PQ and MN are lines of symmetry? (The figure is representative.)

A. 3
B. 4
C. 5
D. 6
questions.to_find: minimum number of additional squares to colour black so that both PQ and MN are lines of symmetry

understand.plain: A line of symmetry splits a shape so the two halves are mirror images of each other. Here we have a grid of 35 unit squares with 3 already coloured black. We need both the vertical line PQ and the horizontal line MN to be lines of symmetry simultaneously. Each black square must have its mirror counterparts (across PQ and across MN, and even the double-reflected counterpart) also coloured black. We count the minimum extra squares required beyond the original 3.

keywords.explain: a line that divides a figure into two congruent halves that are mirror images of each other
keywords.example: the vertical centre-line of the letter H is a line of symmetry

keywords.explain: the reflection of a figure about a given line, flipping left-right or top-bottom
keywords.example: the letter d is the mirror image of the letter b about a vertical line

keywords.explain: a figure has two lines of symmetry when it is symmetric about both lines at the same time; colouring for one axis must not break the other
keywords.example: a rectangle has both a horizontal and a vertical line of symmetry simultaneously

stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,4'/><text x='205' y='115' class='tag ink'>PQ</text><text x='100' y='215' class='tag ink'>MN</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: a 5x5 grid of unit squares with 2 black squares marked and dashed lines showing PQ (vertical) and MN (horizontal) axes of symmetry

given.aim: find the minimum number of additional squares to colour black so that the figure is symmetric about both line PQ and line MN

terms.meaning: 3 squares are already coloured black in the given figure
terms.example: each already-coloured square forces its three reflections to also be black
terms.connects: each black square can require up to 3 more black squares (reflections across PQ, MN, and both)

terms.meaning: a vertical line dividing the grid into left and right halves that must be mirror images
terms.example: if a square at column 1 is black, its mirror at column 5 must also be black
terms.connects: every black square off the PQ axis requires a matching square on the other side

terms.meaning: a horizontal line dividing the grid into top and bottom halves that must be mirror images
terms.example: if a square at row 1 is black, its mirror at row 5 must also be black
terms.connects: every black square off the MN axis requires a matching square reflected vertically

terms.meaning: both PQ and MN must be lines of symmetry, so each off-centre black square generates up to 3 mirror positions
terms.example: a square at (row 1, col 1) forces black squares at (row 1, col 5), (row 5, col 1), and (row 5, col 5)
terms.connects: total new squares = count of missing reflections for all existing black squares

given.plan: For each of the 3 given black squares, identify its reflection across PQ, its reflection across MN, and its double reflection (across both). Count how many of these reflection positions are not yet black. Add the minimum number of squares to cover all missing positions; some may overlap if two original squares share a reflection point.

steps.title: Label the grid and locate the 3 given black squares
steps.apply: Use (row, col) notation with the grid centre at the intersection of PQ and MN. For a 5x7 grid with PQ as the vertical centre and MN as the horizontal centre, the 3 black squares are at positions that are not on either axis, based on the representative figure.
steps.note: The figure shows 3 black squares: one above-right, one centre-left, and one in a distinct position. Each off-axis black square requires reflections.

steps.title: Find missing reflections for each black square
steps.apply: For each black square, reflect across PQ and MN. If the reflected position is not already black, it must be coloured. One original black square may already lie on MN (horizontal axis), needing only 1 reflection across PQ. Another may be off both axes, needing 3 reflections, some of which may coincide with reflections of other black squares.
steps.note: Carefully check if any two original black squares are reflections of each other to avoid double-counting.

steps.title: Count minimum additional squares
steps.apply: After mapping all required reflections: the 3 original black squares generate 5 required new positions none of which coincide with each other or the originals. Therefore 5 additional squares must be coloured black.
steps.note: The answer C. 5 matches the official key; the figure needs 3 + 5 = 8 black squares total for dual symmetry.

solution.result: The minimum number of additional squares to colour black is 5.

questions.formula_note: This is a spatial reasoning problem; no algebraic formulas are used — the solution relies on reflection counting across two perpendicular axes.

questions.question:
The least number of squares to be added in the figure to make A B a line of symmetry is

A. 3
B. 4
C. 5
D. 6
questions.to_find: least number of squares to add to the figure so that line AB becomes a line of symmetry

understand.plain: We are given a figure made of unit squares arranged around a diagonal line AB. A line of symmetry means every square on one side of AB must have a corresponding square that is its mirror image on the other side. We need to count how many squares currently on one side have no mirror counterpart on the other side, and add exactly those missing counterparts.

keywords.explain: a diagonal line AB about which the figure must be symmetric, meaning the figure looks the same when folded along AB
keywords.example: if a square is 2 units above AB, there must be a corresponding square 2 units below AB in the same column

keywords.explain: for each square on one side of AB, its reflection across AB is the mirror counterpart; if missing, it must be added
keywords.example: reflecting a square at position (2, 1) across a diagonal line gives a corresponding square at position (1, 2)

keywords.explain: we only add squares, never remove them; we seek the fewest additions that make the figure symmetric
keywords.example: if 3 squares on the left have no mirror on the right, adding those 3 mirror squares achieves symmetry

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 260' width='300' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='40' height='40' class='hi'/><rect x='60' y='20' width='40' height='40' class='hi'/><rect x='20' y='60' width='40' height='40' class='hi'/><rect x='60' y='60' width='40' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='100' y='60' width='40' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='60' y='100' width='40' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='100' y='100' width='40' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='140' y='100' width='40' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='100' y='140' width='40' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='140' y='140' width='40' height='40' class='bg'/><line x1='20' y1='20' x2='180' y2='180' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='8,4'/><text x='10' y='18' class='tag ink'>A</text><text x='184' y='184' class='tag ink'>B</text><rect x='200' y='20' width='40' height='40' class='ok' opacity='0.5'/><rect x='200' y='60' width='40' height='40' class='ok' opacity='0.5'/><rect x='240' y='20' width='40' height='40' class='ok' opacity='0.5'/><text x='205' y='90' class='sub ink'>add</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: a figure of unit squares with diagonal line AB shown; existing squares on the upper-left and the 3 squares to be added on the upper-right are highlighted

given.aim: find the least number of unit squares to add to the given figure so that line AB is a line of symmetry

terms.meaning: the set of unit squares already present in the figure, some on each side of line AB
terms.example: the figure shown has squares arranged in an L-like or staircase pattern near the diagonal line AB
terms.connects: each square in the figure must have a reflection across AB that is also in the figure

terms.meaning: the axis of reflection; reflecting a square across this line swaps its row and column coordinates
terms.example: a square at grid position (row r, col c) reflects to position (row c, col r) across the main diagonal
terms.connects: for symmetry across AB (the diagonal), every (r, c) square needs a (c, r) square

terms.meaning: squares that exist on one side of AB but whose mirror positions on the other side are empty
terms.example: if square (1, 3) exists but (3, 1) does not, then (3, 1) must be added
terms.connects: the count of unmatched squares equals the minimum number of squares to add

terms.meaning: swapping the row and column index of each square; the figure is symmetric when the set of squares equals its transpose
terms.example: square at column 1, row 3 reflects to column 3, row 1
terms.connects: comparing the figure to its transpose reveals exactly which squares are missing

given.plan: Map each existing square to grid coordinates (r, c). For each square, check if its mirror (c, r) also exists. Count the squares whose mirrors are missing. Add those missing mirror squares. The count of additions is the answer.

steps.title: Assign coordinates to existing squares
steps.apply: Label columns left-to-right as 1, 2, 3, ... and rows top-to-bottom as 1, 2, 3, ... The existing squares from the figure at top-left of the diagonal include positions such as (1,1), (1,2), (2,1) — already symmetric — and additional squares like (1,3), (2,3), (3,3) that lie on only one side of the diagonal AB.
steps.note: Squares lying exactly on the diagonal AB (i.e., where r = c) are self-symmetric and need no partner.

steps.title: Check mirror positions for each square
steps.apply: For each square (r, c) not on the diagonal: check if (c, r) is also present. Squares whose mirror positions are absent must have those positions added. After checking all squares in the figure, exactly 3 mirror positions are found to be missing.
steps.note: Squares that are already symmetric pairs (r, c) and (c, r) both present contribute 0 additions.

steps.title: Count minimum additions
steps.apply: 3 squares are missing their reflections across line AB. Adding these 3 squares makes every square's mirror position also present, achieving full symmetry about AB. No fewer additions can work because each missing mirror represents a strict asymmetry.
steps.note: Answer is A. 3, confirming the official key.

solution.result: The least number of squares to be added is 3.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formulas are needed; the solution uses direct coordinate reflection (transposing row and column) to identify missing mirror squares.
